answer:
Very worried. This is an existential dependency: undocumented DOM, single browser, single acquisition channel, and YouTube ships UI changes constantly — often A/B tested per-user, so breakage is partial and hard to detect. Google also has history of hostility to extensions that overlay YouTube (policy takedowns, Manifest V3 restrictions). Expect breakage multiple times per year; expect a possible permanent kill (policy or redesign) with weeks of notice or none.

What to do, in priority order:

1. **Detect breakage fast.** Automated canary: headless browser loads YouTube hourly, asserts your selectors resolve. Plus client-side telemetry — extension reports selector-miss rate. Target: know within 1 hour, not when signups drop.
2. **Ship fixes fast.** Chrome Web Store review can take days. Mitigate: keep selectors in remotely-fetched config so you can hot-patch without store review (allowed if logic stays in the extension; don't fetch executable code — MV3 violation).
3. **Harden selectors.** Use multiple fallback strategies (aria labels, text content, structural heuristics), not single class names. YouTube's polymer class names churn; aria/roles churn less.
4. **Check official APIs.** YouTube Data API + IFrame Player API cover a lot. Anything you can move off DOM scraping, move.
5. **Diversify acquisition — the real fix.** 80% from one fragile channel is the actual risk. Set a 12-month target (e.g., <50%): SEO, direct product loops, partnerships. Treat extension revenue as funding the transition, not the endgame.
6. **Policy risk audit.** Reread Chrome Web Store and YouTube ToS. If you're injecting UI into YouTube pages, you're also exposed to a store takedown, not just DOM drift — that's the scenario that kills you overnight. Have a Firefox/Edge build and a web-app fallback ready.

Cheap insurance (1–2): days of work. Do those this week. Item 5 is the strategic answer.
